old_claim.note: Advanced Metallurgy (Medieval) provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains because the historical metallurgy lineage already flows through steel_production; adding medieval metallurgy directly duplicates and broadens the causal path.

source_shape.source_locator: British Stainless Steel Association article anchors the 1913 discovery to Brearley's chromium steel composition and surrounding modern alloy work, not to medieval metallurgy as a direct prerequisite.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The relevant direct lineage is steelmaking plus chromium alloy chemistry; medieval metallurgy is too broad and indirect for this edge.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports removal because steel_production already carries earlier metallurgy lineage, avoiding duplicated broad prerequisites on the stainless-steel node.

ontology_before: The graph made stainless steel depend directly on both steel production and medieval advanced metallurgy.
ontology_after: The graph keeps steel production as the direct metallurgy path and removes the redundant medieval-metallurgy edge.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: direct stainless_steel -> advanced_metallurgy_medieval edge removed.
would_reject_if: The steel_production prerequisite is removed from stainless_steel.
A source demonstrates a specific medieval metallurgical method directly required by Brearley's stainless steel.
short_reason: The medieval metallurgy edge duplicated the steel-production lineage and was too broad.
